title: Neonatal blood culture collection
executiveSummary: Draw a blood culture in newborns weighing 500 g to 2500 g (days 0–30) prior to antibiotic administration, provided it does not delay its initiation, ensuring a weight-adjusted volume, strict aseptic technique, and the use of pediatric bottles.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Confirm the clinical indication (suspected sepsis, thermal instability, respiratory distress, NEC, catheter-associated infection); identify the newborn (using two identifiers), weigh, and record the time.
2. Explain the procedure to the family; maintain thermoregulation throughout the process.
3. Prepare pediatric bottles (aerobic and anaerobic according to protocol); disinfect the rubber stoppers with 70% alcohol for ≥ 15 s and allow to dry.
4. Perform hand hygiene (WHO Moment 2); don sterile gloves if performing the puncture through a catheter.
5. Select the site: peripheral venipuncture is preferred; use a UVC/UAC only under a differential time to positivity (DTP) protocol; do not combine catheter and peripheral blood into the same culture set unless explicitly indicated.
6. Disinfect the skin or catheter hub using an antiseptic with friction for ≥ 30 s, extending the area by 2–3 cm; do not re-wipe the disinfected zone.
7. Extract the required volume according to weight (see table) using a high-precision syringe; do not overfill the pediatric bottles.
8. Inoculate the bottles following system guidelines: vacuum-driven systems (aerobic first); syringe-driven systems (anaerobic first, if applicable); mix by gentle inversion.
9. Label the bottles on a clean surface with the newborn's name, weight, date, time, puncture site, set number, and any prior antibiotics administered.
10. In severe sepsis: **do not delay antibiotic therapy** due to difficult venous access; draw cultures in parallel and administer empirical therapy as prescribed.
11. Transport to the laboratory immediately; record the initiation time of the empirical antibiotic.
12. Communicate any positive results, probable contamination (Corynebacterium, Bacillus, Micrococcus), or clinical deterioration.

## Scientific rationale

### Population and scope

**Newborns day 0–30**, **500 g–2500 g**. Early-onset sepsis (< 72 h) and late-onset sepsis (> 72 h) require a blood culture prior to starting antibiotics whenever clinically safe; in septic shock, antibiotic administration must not be delayed.

### Indications

- Clinical suspicion of neonatal sepsis (thermal instability, lethargy, apnea, metabolic acidosis).
- Fever or hypothermia in newborns with maternal risk factors (chorioamnionitis, PROM > 18 h).
- Suspected catheter-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI).
- Suspected or confirmed necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC).
- Poor clinical course in at-risk newborns without an identified source of infection.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Source:** SATI / Critical Care Infectology Consensus — Blood culture contamination has a high impact on neonates; strict aseptic technique and adequate sample volume optimize diagnostic yield (SATI, 2007; updated 2025).

> **Source:** VON — Sepsis is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in VLBW infants; drawing blood cultures prior to antibiotic administration increases diagnostic sensitivity without delaying treatment in severe cases (Vermont Oxford Network).

### Volume according to neonatal weight

| Weight | Volume per bottle | Number of sets |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 500–749 g | 0.5–1 ml | 1–2 (depending on severity) |
| 750–999 g | 1 ml | 1–2 |
| 1000–1499 g | 1–2 ml | 2 in severe sepsis |
| 1500–2500 g | 2–3 ml | 2 in severe sepsis |

Always adhere to the manufacturer's maximum volume threshold for pediatric bottles.

### Timing and site selection

- **Ideal:** Prior to the first antibiotic dose; matching a fever spike is not required.
- **Severe sepsis:** 2 sets from different sites (peripheral + catheter with differential culture if applicable).
- **Neonatal endocarditis:** 3 sets from separate anatomical sites.

### Catheter vs. peripheral blood cultures

| Clinical situation | Action |
| --- | --- |
| Suspected CLABSI | 1 peripheral set + 1 catheter differential culture (matching volumes) |
| Recently placed catheter (< 48 h) | Peripheral site preferred |
| No peripheral access available | Draw from UVC using aseptic technique; document the source |

### Prevention of contamination

- Do not touch the bottle stopper after disinfection.
- Do not use the same alcohol swabs on the skin and the bottle simultaneously (cross-contamination prevention).
- In extreme preterm infants: consider coordinating the puncture during a brief, managed respiratory pause to minimize movement.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Inability to obtain a sample accompanied by clinical deterioration (escalate to UVC or intraosseous access).
- Hemorrhage or extensive hematoma at the puncture site.
- Air embolization during catheter sampling.
- Positive culture results paired with clinical decline (adjust antibiotic therapy immediately).

bibliography.citation: SATI — Microbiological sample collection in the ICU (Critical Infectology Committee). Revision 2007.
Cornistein W, et al. Central venous catheter-associated infections — SATI/SADI consensus 2025.
Vermont Oxford Network. Sepsis prevention and early recognition in the NICU.
World Health Organization. Guidelines on prevention of healthcare-associated infections in neonatal units.
American Academy of Pediatrics. Management of neonatal sepsis and febrile neonates.
